strategic_profile.executive_summary: Yeshiva University is a premier Modern Orthodox Jewish institution in New York City that offers a rigorous dual curriculum, blending traditional Torah study (Torah Umadda) with comprehensive liberal arts, sciences, and pre-professional programs. With distinct undergraduate campuses for men (Yeshiva College/Sy Syms) and women (Stern College for Women/Sy Syms), it provides an immersive academic and religious experience. The university is known for its strong community, high professional placement rates, and robust graduate programs in law, medicine, and psychology.

analyst_takeaways.insight: Students are required to engage in a dual curriculum integrating secular degrees and intensive Jewish studies.
analyst_takeaways.implication: Applicants must be prepared for a highly rigorous, time-intensive academic schedule that functions as the equivalent of two full undergraduate programs.

analyst_takeaways.insight: The overall acceptance rate fell from 63.7% in 2023 to 55.6% in 2024, accompanied by a 53% surge in transfer applications during the spring of 2024.
analyst_takeaways.implication: Yeshiva University is becoming more competitive, functioning as a top safe-haven choice for Jewish students transferring from secular institutions amidst rising campus tensions nationwide.

analyst_takeaways.insight: Practically 100% of the undergraduate student body identifies as Jewish, heavily concentrated in the Modern Orthodox tradition.
analyst_takeaways.implication: The campus environment revolves deeply around Jewish observance, kosher dining, and religious scheduling, making it a highly tailored cultural fit.

analyst_takeaways.insight: Despite a high total Cost of Attendance (over $74,000), 87% of students receive aid with an average grant of approximately $31,000.
analyst_takeaways.implication: The sticker price is rarely the actual cost paid by families, as the university aggressively discounts tuition through institutional need-based and merit aid.

campus_dynamics.social_environment: Deeply tight-knit, values-centered community spanning four campuses in Manhattan and the Bronx. Extensive religious programming, 150+ student clubs, and NCAA Division III athletics (Maccabees) shape the student experience.
campus_dynamics.transportation_impact: Urban campuses are integrated into New York City, serviced by public transportation and a university shuttle system operating between the uptown Wilf campus and midtown Beren campus.
campus_dynamics.research_impact: Robust undergraduate research opportunities, particularly in pre-medical and health sciences fields, with strong ties to the Albert Einstein College of Medicine and the Katz School of Science and Health.

current_status.test_policy_details: Standardized tests are generally optional, but SAT/ACT scores are highly recommended for Honors programs and explicitly required for Early Admission candidates (High School Juniors).

longitudinal_trends.notes: Waitlist data not publicly disclosed. University officials reported the 'longest waitlist we've ever had' in May 2024 due to unprecedented transfer demand.
Transfer acceptance rate hit 100% (55/55 applicants) for the Class of 2027.

gpa.notes: Applicants generally need to be at or above average in their high school class. High School Average equivalent requirement is at least an 85.

testing.policy_note: A minimum SAT score of 1340 or ACT of 29 is required for high school juniors applying for Early Admission.

colleges.name: Yeshiva College
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Male undergraduate students pursuing a liberal arts and sciences education alongside intensive Torah study.
colleges.housing_profile: Located at the Wilf Campus in Washington Heights, Manhattan.
colleges.student_archetype: Pre-med, Humanities, and Sciences track men.

majors.name: Philosophy
majors.notes: Highly rigorous philosophical program frequently engaging directly with Jewish textual interpretation.

majors.name: Biology / Biological Sciences
majors.notes: A leading pre-medical major on campus; Yeshiva boasts a 92% medical school acceptance rate over recent years.

majors.name: Psychology
majors.notes: The most widely pursued undergraduate major by sheer volume of graduates.

colleges.name: Stern College for Women
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Female undergraduate students pursuing arts, sciences, and pre-professional fields alongside Torah study.
colleges.housing_profile: Located at the Beren Campus in Midtown Manhattan.
colleges.student_archetype: Driven female scholars interested in humanities, education, and pre-health.

colleges.name: Sy Syms School of Business
colleges.strategic_fit_advice: Students pursuing high-finance, accounting, and business analytics in New York City.
colleges.housing_profile: Operates classes at both the Wilf and Beren Campuses.
colleges.student_archetype: Future Wall Street professionals, entrepreneurs, and accountants.

majors.name: Finance
majors.notes: Extremely strong pipeline to bulge-bracket investment banks in New York City.

majors.name: Accounting
majors.notes: Highly recruited major by Big 4 accounting firms (Deloitte, PwC, EY).

application_deadlines.notes: Early Decision applicants agree to attend if accepted.
Applications received after this are considered on a space-available rolling basis.

supplemental_requirements.details: Upload response as a PDF. Option 1: Hakarat Hatov - Write a thank you letter to someone in your life. Option 2: Here's how I intend to change the world...
Must have an unweighted high school average of 95 and standardized test scores (Avg: 1450 SAT / 32 ACT).

primary_factors: Rigor of high school academic record
secondary_factors: Contributions to school and community life
holistic_factors.specific_differentiators: The required interview is highly weighted. Admissions officers utilize it heavily to gauge an applicant's readiness for the taxing dual curriculum and their fit within the orthodox Jewish community culture.

scholarships.name: Dean's Scholarship
scholarships.benefits: Renewable for up to four years.

scholarships.name: Anne Scheiber Scholarship
scholarships.benefits: Awarded to women at Stern College planning to study medicine/sciences. Requires minimum 3.25 GPA.

scholarships.name: NYS Scholarships for Academic Excellence
scholarships.benefits: Renewable for four years. Based on Regents exam scores.

scholarships.name: Milken Scholars Scholarship
scholarships.benefits: Access to mentoring and professional development. Requires minimum 3.6 GPA and 1350 SAT / 29 ACT.

ap_policy.general_rule: A score of 4 or 5 earns credit and/or exemption for the equivalent course.
exceptions: No credit is given for AP English Literature.
Advanced Computer Science credit requires passing an internal Python 3 exam at the 80% level.
ap_policy.usage: Can be used to fulfill major and general education requirements, but a maximum residency requirement of 6 semesters on campus still applies.

ib_policy.general_rule: Not explicitly standardized, reviewed on a case-by-case basis alongside AP.

transfer_articulation.restrictions: Transfer students must complete a minimum of 60 credits and 4 semesters on campus. Sixty percent of the major/minor must be completed at the university.

what_it_takes: Ability to handle a highly rigorous dual curriculum (secular studies + intensive religious texts).
A successful alumni interview that demonstrates maturity, strong character, and religious alignment.
Demonstrated excellence in high school with strong standardized test scores, heavily offsetting any transcript weaknesses.
common_activities: Torah study and intensive yeshiva/seminary gap years in Israel
Student government and Honors Leadership Society
essay_tips: Lean into authenticity and gratitude for the 'Hakarat Hatov' prompt; avoid cliché thank-you notes and focus on genuine personal growth.
For the 'change the world' prompt, emphasize community betterment, leadership, and actionable values rather than vague idealism.
red_flags: Lack of enthusiasm or inability to articulate why you want a dual secular/religious curriculum during the interview.
Subpar performance in pre-requisite religious studies if attending a Jewish day school prior.
insights: Athletics scheduling is uniquely intense due to the dual curriculum—practices routinely occur extremely late at night (e.g., 11:00 PM) or early morning (6:00 AM).
The university maintains an extremely loyal and tight-knit alumni network across major NYC law, accounting, and finance firms.
There is a massive emphasis on a gap year in Israel; many students study abroad in Israel for their first year through the S. Daniel Abraham Israel Program before arriving on the NYC campuses.
